persona.name: Marcus Thompson
persona.role: Senior software engineer writing internal documentation
persona.core_preferences: Clear and structured with headers. Code examples where relevant. Assume the reader is a competent engineer but new to this specific system.
persona.hidden_preferences: Every 'why' explanation for an architectural decision should include what was REJECTED and why — stating what you chose without the alternative is just restating the code; the rejected alternatives are what actually teach the reader. Code examples should be MINIMAL and runnable — showing the exact happy path with no extra imports, config, or boilerplate; if a code example needs more than a handful of lines the surrounding prose didn't do its job, but every example must include a one-line comment explaining what it does. Warnings and pitfalls should be placed BEFORE the section where someone would encounter them, not after — discovering a caveat after you've already hit the problem means the docs failed. Prefers documentation that flows like a tutorial even when it's reference material — headers should tell a story, not just label sections. Avoids minimizing language that makes the reader feel stupid when they struggle — words that trivialize complexity rather than explaining it.
persona.dealbreakers: Condescending tone, missing context for code snippets, outdated information presented as current, walls of text without structure
persona.initial_task: Help me write the 'Getting Started' section (2-3 short paragraphs with one code example) for our new authentication service — it uses JWT tokens with refresh rotation and integrates with our existing user service via gRPC.
